title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishery for children only By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News Published: June 27, 2005 Last Modified: June 27, 2005 at 01:51 AM The timing was nearly perfect. Mat Frank and his family moved from the Seattle area to Anchorage only two months ago, and already his kids were fishing on a sun-soaked afternoon in one of the city's urban streams. Does it get any better? Not to Frank. "I used to fish a long time ago," Frank said at midday Sunday sitting on the banks of Campbell Creek below Dimond Boulevard as his three kids baited hooks and dropped their lines. "That's why we came here. Down there, the fishing was laughable." Saturday and Sunday, Frank's children -- Erskine, 12, Cecilia, 10, and Duncan, 8 -- fished with untold numbers of other kids during the first-ever youth-only fishery for Camp…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: 47.3-pound salmon continues to lead derby Published: June 8, 2005 Last Modified: June 8, 2005 at 01:00 AM WASILLA -- Palmer resident Ronda Hausser continued to lead the Mat-Su King Salmon Derby for a second week in a row. Hausser pulled in a 47.3-pound king from the Little Susitna River, according to derby officials. Hausser has already won a $100 barbecue grill from Lowe's in Wasilla, and a $75 gift certificate from Alaska Industrial Hardware and $50 gift certificate from Great Bear Brewing Co. The derby, which runs from May 20 to July 13, includes multiple weekly prizes as well as an overall prize of $10,000 for the biggest king salmon caught. The Wasilla Chamber of Commerce sponsors the derby. -- Daily News staff reports related more Send us your fishing pics Got a photo of yourself or …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing Published: May 29, 2005 Last Modified: May 29, 2005 at 01:12 AM Deshka River waters are up but so is the limit on kings The Deshka River is high and rising, but there's good news for anglers anyway -- Fish and Game has doubled the daily bag and possession limits for king salmon. Anticipating a banner run, the state agency boosted the daily limit from one to two kings, increased the possession limit from two to four and lifted a night-time fishing closure that had been in effect from the mouth to a marker near Chijuk Creek. The changes could jump the annual catch from about 7,000 kings, the average, to 12,000 or more. Sylvia Willis at the Deshka River Lodge expects a busy Memorial Day weekend. "They have been catching fish,'' Willis said. "I wouldn't call it hot, but there's definit…

title: Fishing for controversy: Readers weigh in on beads
summary: When it comes to catching fish, salmon-egg imitations do the job Glo bugs are deadly patterns used on rivers when salmon are spawning, attracting rainbow trout and Dolly Varden. Short of using real salmon eggs, egg-pattern flies are successful time and again. There's nothing wrong with using the right stuff to catch fish. After all, imitation is the name of the game. I often use beads over tied egg patterns. Point in fact, Glo bugs are materials used and tied directly on the hook. Voracious rainbows and Dollies swallow that type of fly down the gullet, and there is a mortality rate that goes with fishing flies such as Glo bugs. Using beads above the hook is equally effective and less damaging to the fish. I allow the bead to free-float above the hook, with a 12-to-14-inch run from the spli…

title: Two die in McKinley fall
summary: ANCHORAGE -- Twin brothers who likely reached the summit of North Americas highest mountain died in a fall, a Denali National Park and Preserve official said Wednesday. It is believed that 55-year-old brothers Jerry and Terry Humphrey of Negley, Ohio, reached the Mount McKinley summit and were descending when one of them fell, perhaps falling into the other, said spokeswoman Maureen McLaughlin. The deaths were the first of the 2005 climbing season on the 20,320-foot mountain in central Alaska. One person died on McKinley last year after being hit by a falling rock. The brothers were not roped together, McLaughlin said. The bodies were found at 17,300 feet, just below Denali Pass. Since 1980, the steep Denali Pass traverse has been the site of six previous deadly falls, McLaughlin said. Th…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Too bad Dad isn't around to check out hunting skills Published: September 25, 2005 Last Modified: September 25, 2005 at 05:03 AM My old man was the best damn wingshot I ever met. And that was the curse of my youth. Or maybe that wasn't a curse so much as his impatience and lack of tolerance for ineptitude. The old man is long dead now. Cancer got him like so many others in the family. But the memories of his shooting come fresh every fall with the dying of the grass. Dead grass has a sweet, rotting smell -- the smell of bird shooting. Today, it wafts around Labradors and mallards. Back then, it was Brittany or springer spaniels and pheasants. "Freckles,'' the Brittany of my pre-teen and early teenage years, was the best. He could go from tearing through the Minnesota bluestem…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Better headlamps brighten darkening skies Published: August 28, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:27 AM The remnants of the first fall storm to sweep Southcentral Alaska were gusting across the Anchorage Hillside when I left the house to get the dogs out of their kennels for an evening run. The start of the season of howling winds brewed in the turbulent North Pacific had arrived unusually early to denude the elderberry bushes and savage weaker trees. Branches and leaves littered the yard. Outside the weather was warm, but the eternal sunshine of the near-arctic summer was clearly giving way to the oncoming blackness of winter. Almost as an afterthought, I grabbed a headlamp to help light the way if we were late coming back down the trail that runs into a valley behi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Climber's mistake leaves him with no money or ID MCKINLEY: Italian cached his items, didn't expect so much snow. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: May 14, 2005 Last Modified: May 14, 2005 at 03:57 AM High on the slopes of Mount McKinley, Italian climber David Bergamin made a tiny mistake that can prove costly, even deadly, in the harsh environment surrounding the continent's tallest peak. Safely back in Anchorage now, Bergamin finds himself a temporarily penniless and unidentifiable foreigner, but he's lucky. He's alive. Two Ohio climbers on Tuesday lost their lives on the mountain because of what appears to be something as simple as a slip or stumble. National Park Service rangers are still trying to determine exactly how 55-year-old identical twin brothers Jerry and Terry H…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Forget your fat dog; Iams wants to talk to you Published: February 13, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 05:55 AM Breathes there a yuppie puppy not spoiled rotten already? If so, help is on the way from the Iams Company, a division of Procter & Gamble. The marketing geniuses who convinced Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race champ Rick Swenson to wear that horrid, Pepto-Bismol pink jumpsuit are rolling out a line of pup-dilly-icious "canine condiments'' to -- as they put it -- "spoil your dog healthy.'' What, you ask, are "canine condiments''? Well, to be honest, I haven't tasted them. But if the marketing is to be believed, we're talking bacon grease and gravy. If, of course, bacon grease and gravy were expensive. "The sauces,'' according to The New York Times, "come in plastic bottles in t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Meat, not antlers, is what Tanana Flats hunters are after MOOSE: 1,300 from Unit 20A could be weighing down boats, heading for freezers. By TIM MOWRY Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: September 8, 2005 Last Modified: September 8, 2005 at 01:24 AM FAIRBANKS -- The spitting rain and chilly breeze at Chena Pump Landing on a late August morning didn't bother Mike Jenkins and Ken Butler as they loaded up their 17-foot Roughneck aluminum johnboat. "The weather could be better, but we'll take it," said the redheaded, pony-tailed Butler. Decked out in full camouflage and holding permits to shoot cow moose on the Tanana Flats south of Fairbanks, the two construction workers from the Matanuska Valley had made the 300-mile drive north in hopes of filling their freezers with moose meat. "I'd prefe…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Troopers cite three fishermen for snagging salmon KENAI RIVER: Authorities say the problem is growing, they're trying to crack down. The Associated Press Published: August 13, 2005 Last Modified: August 13, 2005 at 01:24 AM KENAI -- After spending about three hours observing the three men from the woods, the troopers began writing citations. When they were done, the men each had been cited for failing to release snagged salmon in what authorities say is a growing problem on the Kenai River. Alaska state troopers from the Bureau of Wildlife Enforcement last week cited Jurg G. Jenni, 45, Hans-Peter Lehman, 59, and Patrick Gerber, 31, all of Switzerland. Gerber also was charged with taking an overlimit of salmon. The troopers seized 18 fish, as well as the men's fishing gear. Trooper Dan Dahl…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: First cold trip after steelheads led to a lifetime pursuit By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: October 16, 2005 Last Modified: October 21, 2005 at 11:36 AM ANCHOR RIVER -- Many winters have passed since that very first steelhead trip when, on an icy October morning, I searched the heart of a coastal river for clues to a riddle that comes and goes with the tides. The picnic tables in the parking area glittered with frost as I drove in with pal Paul Cyr. When we stepped from the pickup cab, where it had been warm and smelled of coffee and convenience-store pastries, the sharp air hit me right on the nose. For a moment I considered hopping back in, slamming the door and putting off fishing until spring. But young men in search of steelhead don't own that kind of patience. So we shout…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: World's biggest halibut are caught near Dutch Harbor By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: September 18, 2005 Last Modified: September 18, 2005 at 07:31 AM Out near the end of the Alaska Peninsula, where the Bering Sea battles the North Pacific Ocean across the barrier of the Aleutian Islands, big fish roam. Lurking in the depths are Pacific halibut the size of grizzly bears. Halibut charter boat skippers refer to them as "tabletops" in reference to their size and shape. The biggest tabletop ever landed by an angler would easily seat six. That oval-shaped flatfish measured more than 8 feet long and weighed 459 pounds. It was hauled aboard a Dutch Harbor-based charter by Fairbanks angler Jack Tragis nine years ago. It unseated a 395-pounder caught the year before for the world rec…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: What's in a bead? Just an angler out for a good time By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: September 4, 2005 Last Modified: September 4, 2005 at 04:51 AM Are you one of those anglers who likes to fish, fish, fish and catch nothing? Doubtful. Do you have a child, spouse, relative, friend or acquaintance who likes to fish, fish, fish and catch nothing? Unlikely. Would "The Old Man and the Sea'' have become one of America's great novels if ole Santiago had gone out in the Gulf Stream and caught nothing? Of course not. The purpose of fishing -- not to mention its joy -- is wrapped up in catching fish. Even the holiest of fly anglers in the hair-jacket crowd recognize this, but they just can't seem to countenance the idea that what holds for them should apply to the great unwashed mas…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: To catch trout, have nose for salmon KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: July 28, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 07:26 AM Some of the best-looking trout water produced absolutely nothing earlier this week. Glassy pools with roiling heads and swift tail-outs resembled images from classic fly-fishing paintings, but many offered no fish. Veteran angler Paul Cyr was not surprised. Nor was he concerned. He waded upstream, methodically peering into each slick and eddy for a telltale sign. And when he finally found it 100 yards upstream of where he'd started, he stopped short like a bird dog on point, crouched slightly and started casting. The spot, a piece of water hardly larger than a dining room tabletop, seemed an unlikely choice. The current was fast, part of a sequence of rapids th…

title: Dry-fly-fishing opportunities abound
summary: Anchorage angler Patrick Dougherty arrived at the head of the canyon three hours before dusk, hiking into the shade of its high stone walls to a river that hissed among moss-topped boulders. The air there was cool and humid, ferns sprouted from ledges in the rocks, and somewhere short of the first set of rapids, Dougherty paused at the edge of a pool. Backed by a knot of alders and tall grass, the pool was deep and lit by a stray sunbeam through which a halo of insects swarmed. Eyes on the pool, Dougherty tucked his 7-weight rod under one arm and reached into his vest for a box of flies. Then he did something too rarely witnessed on Southcentral Alaska streams: He tied on a dry fly. A No. 6 Stimulator, to be exact. Next he stripped line from his reel, made a couple of false casts and softl…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Moose hunt poses a threat to wolves DENALI: Researcher says nearby pack would be too easy to pick off. By DOUG O'HARRA Anchorage Daily News Published: August 30, 2005 Last Modified: August 30, 2005 at 01:30 AM A federal subsistence moose hunt set to begin Thursday in the heart of the Denali National Park and Preserve might bring hunters extra close to a wolf pack southeast of Kantishna -- raising old issues about how much protection should be given to the park's most closely studied animal. The pack, with two adult female wolves and one large male, is often seen by visitors to the Kantishna-Wonder Lake area. This summer, the group is raising three pups in an open valley near an old mining trail and would be unusually easy to shoot by subsistence hunters searching for moose along the North …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Seward Derby: 78-year-old from Kasilof hits pay dirt A 17.42-pound silver nets Hoekema $10,000. By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: August 28, 2005 Last Modified: August 28, 2005 at 03:49 AM At 1 p.m. Saturday, with only three hours left of the 50th Seward Silver Salmon Derby, Roy Hoekema tried staying calm at his Kasilof home. The leader of the derby he has entered just about every year since 1981 was poised to win $10,000, but there were still fishermen out in Resurrection Bay seeking something bigger. "I'm living on Tums, and my wife keeps checking the results," said Hoekema, a retired 78-year-old who spends his summers fishing out of one of his two boats. "I was still in the lead about an hour ago." By 4 p.m., the derby was over and once all the fish were weighed, Hoeke…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Blazing a new old trail Once-hidden Winner Creek becoming real path By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: August 21, 2005 Last Modified: August 21, 2005 at 04:12 AM WINNER CREEK -- There was always a trail here, at least in modern times. The problem was finding it. When trekker Clark Saunders led a group up the valley and on through to Winner Creek Pass in 2002, the route started on a wet, muddy path east of the Alyeska Prince Hotel. After crossing the creek, it turned into a decent animal trail that led to a stand of spruce trees with only minimal blowdown before leading down some steep, gnarly alder chutes back to the creek and across it into a tangle of willows. Saunders insisted that somewhere in the ensuing mess of bushwacking was the proverbial "old-horse trail.'' And, in f…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Silver salmon hot spots are made in the shade KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 11, 2005 Last Modified: August 11, 2005 at 03:53 AM WILLOW -- The silvers were so fresh and bright Monday evening that I almost missed seeing them. Their scales reflected the sunlight and made the fish appear translucent, almost invisible. Only their darker, distinctively squared tails against the sandy riverbed gave them away. Bunched up in a tight knot, the school held midstream, hugging the bottom. Imagine a shadow the size of a car top backed by a dozen or more gently undulating tails. Reaching them with a fly rod wasn't easy. Back-cast-snagging willows and alders grew thick along the banks. And the current where they held was swift and deep, forcing me to cast far upstream so the weighted fly coul…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Outdoors hot line Published: August 7, 2005 Last Modified: August 7, 2005 at 04:04 AM BOOK SIGNINGS Author signs copies of book on baby birds Joan Harris, author of "One Wing's Gift," will sign copies of her new book, "The Least of These: Wild Baby Bird Rescue Stories," 1 to 2:30 p.m. Saturday at Title Wave Books, 1360 W. Northern Lights Blvd. For details, call 278-9283. CYCLING Women's group holds clinic, rides trails Join the Women's Mountain Bike and Tea Society, WOMBATS, at 7 p.m. Monday at the Kincaid Stadium trail head for a beginner's skills clinic. Learn the basics of shifting and braking, as well as positioning for ascending and descending hills. For intermediate and advanced riders, meet at 7 p.m. Wednesday at the Chugiak High School poolside parking lot for a ride on the ski tra…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Tides lift silvers into Bird Creek KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 4, 2005 Last Modified: August 7, 2005 at 06:05 AM BIRD CREEK -- The fishing got crazy around 4:30 Monday afternoon, as the incoming tide began its sweep up Turnagain Arm. Where the green-tinted estuary had earlier slithered along the bottom of a slick mud gut, it now flowed deep and was on the way to filling its banks. Salmon rolled everywhere. And for the handful of anglers who braved occasional torrents of blowing, in-your-face rain, the action at this popular Seward Highway fishery 25 miles south of Anchorage was excellent for hard-running, sea-bright silvers. Among those anglers was visiting East Coast fisherman Govinddass Goleman. Dragging a pair of still-flopping silvers, Goleman paused midway across the ne…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: If you go Published: July 18, 2005 Last Modified: July 18, 2005 at 05:29 AM GETTING THERE: The Alaska Rainforest Sanctuary is about eight miles south of Ketchikan on the South Tongass Highway. From the highway, turn left on Wood Road. The sanctuary is less than a mile down the road. RESTRICTIONS: The tour is designed for those in good physical condition. Participants must weigh 80 to 250 pounds and be at least 10 years old. COST: $149 for cruise ship passengers by appointment during the week and $79 on Saturdays for all visitors. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Potter Marsh is the place to go in Anchorage to watch birds By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: July 17, 2005 Last Modified: July 18, 2005 at 03:08 AM Nobody wants to think about it. The days are still warm; the sun still lingers well into the evening. But the birds of Alaska already have winter on their minds, and if you stop and look carefully, there are signs of their retreat everywhere. Migration time is nearly here. And one of the best places to view it is Potter Marsh. "Certainly, activity is starting to wind down, although there are going to be birds there for a couple of months at least," Stan Senner, vice president and executive director of the National Audubon Society's Alaska state office, said of the birds that arrived at the marsh just two months ago. Then, Pot…

summary: Outdoors hot line Published: June 5, 2005 Last Modified: June 5, 2005 at 05:19 AM BIRDING Join a guided, early morning walk in search of birds Take a guided early morning bird walk at 7 a.m. Saturday at the Eagle River Nature Center. Audubon member Sirena Brownlee will lead this walk on nearby trails. Meet on the front porch of the nature center. For questions, call Sirena at 222-7905. Anchorage Audubon hosts walks at local lakes Meet at Goose Lake, off UAA Drive, at 7 a.m. Saturday for a bird walk with Audubon members. Participants will hike along the bike trail on the east side of the lake, looking for the Pacific loons that nest there. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Deadly duty Unpaid rangers sacrifice lives on Mount McKinley By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: October 25, 2003 Last Modified: October 26, 2003 at 12:40 AM Possibly nowhere in government service, outside of combat, have volunteers paid as high a price for public service as the men and women who comprise the ranger patrols on Mount McKinley. Since the Volunteers in the Parks program was formalized in 1979 to help the National Park Service in patrolling North America's tallest peak, 334 climbers have volunteered a month or more of their time to aid search and rescue -- and waste removal -- on the mountain. Four of these volunteers -- about one in every 80 participants -- have ended up making the ultimate sacrifice. Volunteer mountain rangers Brian Reagan and Adam Kolff died whe…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Alaska sports digest: Harrison's 50-pound king weighs in as new derby leader Daily News staff Published: June 11, 2005 Last Modified: June 11, 2005 at 04:48 AM Craig Harrison put a slam in the Slam'n Salm'n Derby at Ship Creek early Friday morning after catching a pro-class leading lunker. Harrison, who's been stationed at Elmendorf Air Force Base since 1999, snagged a 50.20-pounder at 8:15 a.m., blowing away the previous pro class leader, James Jones, who caught a 38.35-pound fish. Overall, the second heaviest fish is Davis Nashalook's 42-pounder in the senior division. "I got lucky," Harrison said. He has until 3 p.m. Sunday to keep the lead and win a trip for two to Ultima Thule Lodge in the Wrangell Mountains valued at approximately $10,000. Slam'n Salm'n Derby Sponsored by Alaska Rail…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing briefs Published: June 5, 2005 Last Modified: June 5, 2005 at 12:36 AM Dipnetters, get ready; fish limit has increased Want salmon? Alaska's Department of Fish and Game says so many red salmon are swarming up the Copper River that dipnetters will get 10 bonus fish for the June 6 to June 12 opener. The extra 10 come on top of the standard limit of 15 salmon per person or 30 per head of household. As always, dipnetters need permits and must record their catches. Only Alaskans who have lived here a year can participate. Kings are in at Ship Creek; bring spinners, bait, corkies Anchorage's favorite salmon fishery, Ship Creek, finally has fish. Alaska Department of Fish and Game area sport fisheries biologist Dan Bosch describes the king fishing on the flood tides as "hot.'' The preferr…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing with beads is akin to laser-guided golf balls By RICHARD CHIAPPONE Special to the Daily News Published: September 4, 2005 Last Modified: September 4, 2005 at 04:51 AM Tired of trying to catch trout that don't want to be caught? Weary of learning to cast a fly and mend the line for a drag-free drift and all that fancy stuff? Well, boys and girls, have I got something great for you. Pegged beads! Yes! With pegged beads there is no longer any need to develop actual fishing skills! Now everyone can catch dozens of trout a day and never strain their brains or their casting arms one bit. With ineffective, old-fashioned trout fishing tactics, first you had to figure out what the fish were eating. Then you had to tie all sorts of yarn and tinsel and bird feathers and the belly fur of Tibet…
